title: Applying liming wax to an oak piece
subtitle: Wire-brush open the grain of an oak surface and work white liming wax into the grain channels for a pale, bleached finish
excerpt: How to apply liming wax to oak furniture or panelling. Covers wire-brushing the grain open, applying and working the wax into the grain channels, removing the excess, and sealing with a clear wax or oil.
sourceNotes: UK furniture and interior finishing practice for liming oak. Traditional Scandinavian and Arts and Crafts period finishing technique. City and Guilds Level 2 Furniture Restoration.

glossaryTerms.definition: A paste wax containing white pigment (traditionally lime (calcium hydroxide) in the nineteenth-century original, now typically zinc white or titanium dioxide in oil or wax carriers. Applied to wire-brushed, open-grained timber such as oak, ash, or elm; the pigment settles into the grain channels and open pores, leaving the face grain largely clear. The result is a pale, bleached appearance that reads white-grey in the grain and the natural timber colour on the raised face grain. A favourite finish for Arts and Crafts and Scandinavian furniture.

content.text: works on oak by sitting white pigment in the open grain channels, giving a pale, bleached character to the surface. The approach is wire-brushing the grain open, working the wax in with a stiff brush or cloth, removing the excess from the face grain, and sealing. The grain must be open) this technique does not work on close-grained timbers such as maple or cherry.

Liming wax requires bare timber (strip any existing finish with the appropriate stripper or by sanding to bare wood. Sand the surface with 80-grit along the grain to clear any previous filler or surface treatment. The surface should feel clean and slightly rough) no smoothed-over grain channels.

Use a coarse brass or steel wire brush and scrub firmly along the grain direction (not across it. The wire bristles lift the soft summer-growth grain and leave the harder winter-growth grain proud, opening the channel. Three to four firm passes along each section is usually enough. Vacuum the brushed dust. The surface should look visibly textured, with open channels running along the grain.

Apply a generous coat of liming wax with a stiff brush or a cloth, working it into the grain channels with a circular motion. Ensure every grain channel is filled) the wax should be visibly sitting in the channels. Allow to sit for 2 to 5 minutes.

Wipe off the excess wax from the face grain with a lint-free cloth, working along the grain. The wax should remain only in the grain channels (the face grain wipes clean. Work each section while the wax is still slightly soft. Wax that has hardened can be re-softened with a damp cloth. Once the grain is filled and the face is clean, buff lightly with a clean cloth.

Apply a clear furniture wax or a liming-wax-compatible sealer over the entire surface to protect the pigmented grain from handling. Do not use an oil finish over liming wax) oils dissolve the wax carrier and wash the pigment out of the grain.
